title: QUIC Local Harness Preflight Planning and Localhost Smoke Architecture
purpose: Describe how the interop harness exposes testable local planning decisions and localhost smoke execution without changing the external runner.
design_summary: The harness gains a small preflight planner that centralizes request parsing, qlog selection, endpoint resolution, transfer-path mapping, and the supported client/server option builders. `InteropHarnessRunner` stays the execution layer: it uses the planner to resolve dispatch decisions, then runs the QUIC client or listener path exactly as before. The new requirement-home tests split along the same boundary: one planner-focused unit test proves the request and endpoint decisions, and a set of in-process localhost smoke tests call `InteropHarnessRunner.Run` directly with temp writers and matching localhost certificate material so handshake, retry, and transfer can be exercised without the external runner.
alternatives_considered: Keep the decision logic inside `InteropHarnessRunner` and add only larger end-to-end tests. Rejected because the plan needs smaller proofs that isolate harness setup from transport behavior.
Run only the external `quic-interop-runner` against quic-go and msquic. Rejected because that still leaves harness planning failures coupled to Docker and the external runner.
edge_cases_and_constraints: The localhost smoke lane must keep hostname and certificate validation strict; `127.0.0.1` must still fail against a localhost-only certificate.
The transfer preflight logic depends on the fixed `/www` and `/downloads` mount roots and should not escape those roots.
Direct in-process smoke tests must run the server and client concurrently without deadlocking the test host.
data_and_state_considerations: Planner instances are short-lived and carry only the immutable harness environment plus the stdout writer used for validation callbacks. qlog capture remains a per-run disposable scope that writes a contained snapshot to the configured directory. The localhost smoke tests use temporary PEM files and qlog directories and clean up any files they create under the fixed mount roots after each run.
risks: Local host path resolution for `/www` and `/downloads` can vary slightly by platform, so the tests should compare resolved full paths rather than raw strings.
In-process runs use dedicated threads and can still hang if a QUIC handshake regresses, so the smoke tests need bounded timeouts and useful failure output.
The localhost-only certificate scenario must not silently fall back to IP-literal acceptance, or the preflight lane stops being a strict isolation signal.

supplemental_sections.heading: Scope
supplemental_sections.content: This design covers the local planning seam and the direct localhost smoke lane only. The external `quic-interop-runner` helper remains a separate slice under REQ-QUIC-INT-0013.
